mappings.rationaleAI: Human ecology and restoration ecology relevant to the influence of the human species on environmental modification (B.1) and the conservation and management of natural resources (D).
Animal cultivation and breeding (C.4) and major uses of animals for food, materials, and labor (C.5).
Management of the world's food supply including difficulties facing developing countries (D.4) and uses of plants for food (C.3).
Maize domesticated ~9,000 years ago as a cultivated plant (C.1) with breeding for yield and disease resistance (C.2).
Agriculture as the cultivation of plants and livestock encompassing domestication (C.1), plant breeding and growing (C.2), and animal breeding and raising (C.4).
Museums of natural history as public collections of preserved animals and plants (C.6.a).
Meat as animal tissue consumed as food, a major use of domesticated animals (C.5).
Domestic cat as a domesticated species (C.1) used for vermin control and companionship (C.5).
Soil as a natural resource type (D.2) affected by human environmental modification including degradation and contamination (B.1).
Ecosystem conservation and management scope (D.1) and management of natural resources (D.3).
Dog as the first domesticated species (C.1) with major uses including herding, hunting, guarding, and companionship (C.5).
Biotechnology including genetic engineering relevant to deliberate genetic modification (B.3) and plant breeding through recombinant DNA (C.2).
Biology as the science of life broadly relevant to qualities setting humans apart in the biosphere (A).
The history of domestication and farming across independent Neolithic centers relevant to the distribution and development of plant and animal domestication (C.1).
Vegetables as cultivated edible plant parts relevant to plant breeding and growing (C.2) and uses of plants (C.3).
Species as the foundational unit of biodiversity relevant to the nature and scope of conservation management (D.1).
Fishing as a major use of aquatic animals (C.5) connected to management of the world's food supply (D.4).
The physical world's phenomena including ecosystems and living organisms, relevant to human influence on environmental modification (B.1).
Animal husbandry as the agricultural management of livestock through feeding, breeding, and health management (C.4).
Domestication as the multi-generational process of controlling species for food, fibre, labour, and companionship (C.1).
Gardens as planned spaces for cultivating plants relevant to plant breeding and growing (C.2) and botanical gardens and arboretums (C.6.d).
Evolutionary change relevant to the influence of the environment on the modification of the human species (B.2).
Botany as the study of plants relevant to the cultivation of plants (C.2) and uses of plants (C.3).
Pig domesticated independently twice, exemplifying animal domestication (C.1) and animal breeding and raising (C.4).
